notes: Both require documented security policies approved by management and subject to regular review.

Security policies inform the governance framework underpinning GDPR records of processing activities.
Both require clearly defined security roles and responsibilities allocated within the organisation.
Both controls address establishing and communicating cybersecurity roles, responsibilities, and authorities.
Designation of a Data Protection Officer under GDPR is a specific role-allocation requirement addressed in the broader ISO 27001 control.
Threat intelligence feeds directly into the risk analysis required under NIS2 Art. 21(2)(a).

NIS2 Art. 21(2)(j) extends access control to require MFA, which is a specific implementation of ISO 27001 5.15.

Both require documented incident management processes covering prevention, detection, response and recovery.
Both mandate establishing an ICT/information security incident management process including detection and response.
Both require establishing an incident response programme covering preparation, detection, containment and eradication.

DORA Art. 19 incident reporting obligations depend on the response processes established under ISO 27001 5.26.
Both cover activities to prevent expansion of an event and mitigate its effects.
Both require conducting post-incident reviews and applying lessons learned to improve security controls.
Both address investigating incidents to improve future response and support forensic and recovery activities.
Both require business continuity planning to maintain security during disruptions including backup and disaster recovery.
Both require a comprehensive ICT/information business continuity policy covering systems availability.

Both address infrastructure resilience and continuity planning to protect asset availability.
Both require data recovery practices sufficient to restore systems to a pre-incident state.
Identifying regulatory requirements (ISO 27001 5.31) supports assessing the effectiveness of NIS2 risk management measures.
Both require cybersecurity awareness training and education for all relevant personnel.

Both require providing personnel with cybersecurity awareness and training for their specific roles.
Both require establishing a security awareness programme to influence workforce behaviour.
Both require malware protection measures to prevent, detect and respond to malicious software.
Both require continuous vulnerability assessment and timely remediation across all IT assets.
Both require ongoing risk and vulnerability assessments of ICT/information systems.

Both require establishing and maintaining secure configuration baselines for all enterprise assets and software.
Both address managing hardware, software and services to maintain confidentiality, integrity and availability.
Both require technical measures to prevent unauthorised access to or leakage of sensitive/personal data.
Both require managing data to protect confidentiality, integrity and availability consistent with the risk strategy.
Both require collecting, retaining, protecting and reviewing audit logs of relevant events.
Both require monitoring mechanisms to promptly detect anomalous activities and ICT incidents.
Both require continuous monitoring of assets for anomalies, indicators of compromise, and adverse events.
Both require network monitoring and defence processes to detect and respond to security threats.
Both require actively managing network infrastructure to prevent exploitation of vulnerable services.
Both address protecting against threats from web browsers and email vectors.
Both require policies and procedures for the appropriate use of cryptography and encryption.
GDPR Art. 32 explicitly mentions encryption as a technical measure for securing personal data.
Both require security in network and information system acquisition, development and maintenance.
Both require security and data protection to be built into systems by design and by default.
Both require managing the security lifecycle of software to prevent, detect and remediate weaknesses.
Both require managing third-party/supply chain security risk as an integral part of the overall risk framework.
Both require evaluating and managing service providers who hold sensitive data or support critical processes.
Both address supply chain risk management as a governed, monitored, and continuously improved process.
Both require ensuring third parties provide sufficient guarantees for security and data protection compliance.
DORA Art. 30 provides specific contractual requirements to implement the third-party risk management framework in Art. 28.
Both require identifying, establishing, and monitoring cyber supply chain risk management processes.
Both require notifying authorities within 72 hours of becoming aware of a significant security/data breach incident.
Both mandate reporting significant incidents to relevant competent authorities within defined timeframes.
Both require timely authority notification of incidents; DORA applies to financial sector ICT incidents, GDPR to personal data breaches.
Both require implementing appropriate technical and organisational security measures to ensure CIA of data and systems.
Both require managing data security to protect confidentiality, integrity and availability against identified risks.
Both require conducting formal risk assessments (DPIA / ICT risk identification) before deploying high-risk processing or systems.
Both require understanding the risk to the organisation, its assets and individuals through structured risk assessment.
Both require a documented ICT/information security risk management framework as the foundation of the organisation's overall risk management.
Both require establishing an organisation-wide risk management strategy with defined risk tolerance and appetite.
Both require maintaining a comprehensive, up-to-date inventory of all enterprise/ICT assets.
Both require identifying and controlling all software assets running within the enterprise environment.
Both require identifying and managing assets that enable business purposes consistent with their relative importance.
Both require analysing anomalous events to detect and characterise cybersecurity/ICT incidents.
Both require restoring operational availability of affected systems following a cybersecurity incident.
Both require recovery capability as part of business continuity and crisis management planning.

BSI IT-Grundschutz ISMS.1 and ISO 27001:2022 5.1 both require a documented information security policy approved by management and subject to regular review within an established ISMS.
BSI IT-Grundschutz ORP.1 and ISO 27001:2022 5.2 both require that information security responsibilities are clearly defined, allocated and communicated throughout the organisation.
BSI IT-Grundschutz ORP.3 and ISO 27001:2022 6.3 both mandate regular security awareness, education and training tailored to the roles and responsibilities of personnel.

BSI IT-Grundschutz ORP.4 and ISO 27001:2022 5.15 both require a defined access control policy and process for granting, reviewing, and revoking user access rights based on the principle of least privilege.
BSI IT-Grundschutz CON.1 and ISO 27001:2022 8.24 both require an organisation-wide cryptography policy that defines approved algorithms, key lengths and key management procedures.

BSI IT-Grundschutz CON.3 and ISO 27001:2022 8.13 both require a documented backup policy covering schedule, retention, storage locations, protection of media, and regular testing of restoration procedures.
BSI IT-Grundschutz DER.1 and ISO 27001:2022 8.16 both require continuous monitoring of systems and networks to detect anomalies, potential security events, and attack patterns in a timely manner.

BSI IT-Grundschutz DER.2.1 and ISO 27001:2022 5.26 both require defined procedures for responding to information security incidents, including classification, escalation, containment, eradication, recovery, and post-incident review.

BSI IT-Grundschutz DER.4 and ISO 27001:2022 5.29 both require documented and tested information security continuity plans that ensure critical functions can be maintained or rapidly restored during a disruption.

BSI IT-Grundschutz NET.1.1 and ISO 27001:2022 8.20 both require that network security is addressed through defined architecture principles, segmentation, and documented controls to protect information in transit.

KRITIS §8a(1) BSIG and ISO 27001:2022 5.1 both require documented security policies; KRITIS is sector-specific and focuses on availability and resilience of critical infrastructure, whereas ISO 27001 provides a broader ISMS policy framework.

KRITIS §8a(1a) BSIG and ISO 27001:2022 8.16 both address continuous monitoring and detection. KRITIS requires specific attack detection systems (Angriffserkennung), while ISO 27001 addresses broader monitoring activities.

KRITIS §8a(3) BSIG and ISO 27001:2022 5.35 both require periodic independent review of security measures. KRITIS mandates proof of compliance to BSI every two years; ISO 27001 requires independent review of the ISMS.

KRITIS §8b(4a) BSIG and ISO 27001:2022 5.26 both address incident response; KRITIS additionally mandates external reporting of significant disruptions to BSI, whereas ISO 27001 focuses primarily on internal incident response procedures.
KRITIS §8a(1) BSIG and NIS2 Art. 21(2)(a) both require risk analysis and state-of-the-art technical and organisational security measures to protect network and information systems.
KRITIS §8b(4a) BSIG and NIS2 Art. 23(1) both impose mandatory incident reporting obligations to the competent national authority without undue delay when significant disruptions occur.
KRITIS §8a(3) BSIG requires periodic proof of compliance provided to BSI; NIS2 Art. 21(2)(f) requires policies and procedures to assess the effectiveness of cybersecurity risk management measures.

NIS2UmsuCG §30 transposes NIS2 Art. 21(2)(a) into German law, requiring essential entities to implement risk analysis policies and information system security measures as part of their risk management obligations.

NIS2UmsuCG §35 transposes NIS2 Art. 23(1) into German law, requiring significant incident notifications to BSI within identical timelines: early warning within 24 hours, detailed notification within 72 hours, and a final report within one month.

NIS2UmsuCG §38 transposes NIS2 Art. 20(1) into German law, requiring management bodies to approve cybersecurity measures, oversee their implementation, and take personal liability for failures in cybersecurity governance.

NIS2UmsuCG §39 transposes NIS2 Art. 21(2)(d) into German law, requiring essential and important entities to address supply chain cybersecurity risks including evaluation of supplier and service provider security practices.

NIS2UmsuCG §30 and KRITIS §8a(1) BSIG both require state-of-the-art technical and organisational security measures. The NIS2UmsuCG broadens the scope beyond critical infrastructure sectors to include important entities, while KRITIS remains focused on critical infrastructure operators.

NIS2UmsuCG §35 and KRITIS §8b(4a) BSIG both impose mandatory incident reporting obligations to BSI. The NIS2UmsuCG introduces structured timelines (24h/72h/1 month) that supplement and supersede the existing KRITIS reporting obligations.

NIS2UmsuCG §38 establishes personal management liability for cybersecurity governance, while KRITIS §8a(3) requires periodic proof of compliance submitted to BSI. Both controls reinforce accountability for security measures at senior management level.

BSI IT-Grundschutz ISMS.1 and NIS2 Art. 21(2)(a) both address information security risk management governance. ISMS.1 provides a comprehensive ISMS process while NIS2 Art. 21(2)(a) focuses specifically on policies for risk analysis and information system security.

BSI IT-Grundschutz DER.2.1 and NIS2 Art. 21(2)(b) both cover incident handling, including detection, reporting, response and recovery. DER.2.1 provides detailed procedural guidance, while NIS2 Art. 21(2)(b) sets the minimum regulatory requirement.

BSI IT-Grundschutz DER.4 and NIS2 Art. 21(2)(c) both address business continuity and crisis management for IT systems. DER.4 provides detailed BCM guidance aligned with BSI standards, while NIS2 Art. 21(2)(c) establishes the regulatory requirement for backup management, disaster recovery, and crisis management.
ISO 27001:2013 A.5.1.1 (information security policies) and NIS2 Art. 21(2)(a) both require documented risk analysis and information system security policies approved by management.

ISO 27001:2013 A.6.1.1 (information security roles and responsibilities) and NIS2 Art. 20(1) both require clearly defined security responsibilities allocated within the organisation and overseen by the management body.

ISO 27001:2013 A.16.1.2 (reporting of information security events) and NIS2 Art. 23(1) both require timely reporting of significant incidents, though NIS2 specifies formal timelines to the national authority.
ISO 27001:2013 A.17.1.1 (planning information security continuity) and NIS2 Art. 21(2)(c) both address business continuity and crisis management as core security requirements.
ISO 27001:2013 A.15.1.1 (information security policy for supplier relationships) and NIS2 Art. 21(2)(d) both address supply chain security including security requirements in supplier agreements.
ISO 27001:2013 A.12.6.1 (technical vulnerability management) and NIS2 Art. 21(2)(e) both address security in the maintenance and acquisition of network and information systems.
ISO 27001:2013 A.7.2.2 (information security awareness, education and training) and NIS2 Art. 21(2)(g) both require basic cyber hygiene practices and regular cybersecurity training for personnel.
ISO 27001:2013 A.10.1.1 (policy on cryptographic controls) and NIS2 Art. 21(2)(h) both require policies and procedures governing the use of cryptography and encryption.

ISO 27001:2013 A.18.1.4 (privacy and protection of personally identifiable information) and GDPR Art. 5(1)(f) both require appropriate security measures to ensure integrity and confidentiality of personal data.

ISO 27001:2013 A.18.1.4 (privacy and PII protection) directly addresses the same security-of-processing obligations as GDPR Art. 32, requiring appropriate technical and organisational measures to protect personal data.

ISO 27001:2013 A.18.1.1 (identification of applicable legislation and contractual requirements) and GDPR Art. 30 (records of processing activities) are related in that A.18.1.1 requires identification of data protection obligations which includes maintaining Art. 30 records.

ISO 27001:2013 A.16.1.2 (reporting information security events) and GDPR Art. 33 (notification of personal data breach) both address incident reporting obligations, with GDPR specifying a 72-hour deadline to the supervisory authority for personal data breaches.

ISO 27001:2013 A.18.1.4 (privacy and PII protection) and GDPR Art. 35 (data protection impact assessment) are related: A.18.1.4 provides the overarching security framework within which DPIAs required by Art. 35 are conducted.

ISO 27001:2013 A.9.1.1 (access control policy) and GDPR Art. 25 (data protection by design and by default) are related: access controls implementing least privilege directly support the Art. 25 requirement to process only necessary personal data by default.

ISO 27001:2013 A.17.1.1 (planning information security continuity) and DORA Art. 11 (response and recovery) both require planning and implementing measures to ensure continuity and timely recovery of information systems following a disruption.

ISO 27001:2013 A.12.3.1 (information backup) and DORA Art. 12 (backup policies and restoration) both require documented backup policies covering backup frequency, retention, storage, and regular testing of restoration procedures.

ISO 27001:2013 A.12.6.1 (management of technical vulnerabilities) and DORA Art. 8 (ICT risk identification) both address the systematic identification and management of vulnerabilities in IT/ICT systems.

ISO 27001:2013 A.16.1.2 (reporting information security events) and DORA Art. 17 (ICT-related incident management process) both require a formal process for reporting and managing information security and ICT incidents.

ISO 27001:2013 A.15.1.1 (information security policy for supplier relationships) and DORA Art. 28 (general requirements for ICT third-party risk management) both require that security risks from third-party service providers are identified and managed.

ISO 27001:2013 A.5.1.1 (policies for information security) and DORA Art. 5 (ICT risk management framework) both require a documented governance framework for managing information/ICT security risks with senior management accountability.
ISO 27001:2013 A.8.1.1 (inventory of assets) and CIS Control 1 (inventory and control of enterprise assets) both require a comprehensive, actively managed inventory of all hardware assets.
ISO 27001:2013 A.12.2.1 (controls against malware) and CIS Control 10 (malware defences) both require the deployment and management of technical controls to detect, prevent and respond to malware.
ISO 27001:2013 A.12.4.1 (event logging) and CIS Control 8 (audit log management) both require the generation, collection, retention and review of security-relevant event logs.

ISO 27001:2013 A.12.6.1 (management of technical vulnerabilities) and CIS Control 7 (continuous vulnerability management) both require a systematic process for identifying, prioritising and remediating technical vulnerabilities.
ISO 27001:2013 A.9.2.3 (management of privileged access rights) and CIS Control 5 (account management) both address the management and control of user and privileged accounts across systems.

ISO 27001:2013 A.7.2.2 (information security awareness, education and training) and CIS Control 14 (security awareness and skills training) both require role-appropriate security awareness and training programmes for all personnel.

ISO 27001:2013 A.16.1.1 (responsibilities and procedures for incident management) and CIS Control 17 (incident response management) both require defined incident response procedures, roles and responsibilities.
ISO 27001:2013 A.13.1.1 (network controls) and CIS Control 12 (network infrastructure management) both require that networks are managed and secured with documented controls.

ISO 27001:2013 A.5.1.1 (policies for information security) and NIST CSF GV.PO (Policy) both require that cybersecurity policy is established, documented, communicated and enforced across the organisation.
ISO 27001:2013 A.8.1.1 (inventory of assets) and NIST CSF ID.AM (Asset Management) both require that assets are inventoried and managed to support risk management decisions.

ISO 27001:2013 A.12.6.1 (technical vulnerability management) and NIST CSF ID.RA (Risk Assessment) are related: systematic vulnerability management provides essential input to the risk assessment process.

ISO 27001:2013 A.16.1.5 (response to information security incidents) and NIST CSF RS.MA (Incident Management) both require defined procedures for responding to and managing confirmed security incidents.

ISO 27001:2013 A.17.1.1 (planning information security continuity) and NIST CSF RC.RP (Incident Recovery Plan Execution) both address the planning and execution of recovery activities following a security disruption.

ISO 27001:2013 A.6.1.1 (information security roles and responsibilities) and NIST CSF GV.RR (Roles, Responsibilities, and Authorities) both require cybersecurity roles and authorities to be clearly defined and allocated.

ISO 27001:2013 A.15.1.1 (information security policy for supplier relationships) and NIST CSF GV.SC (Cybersecurity Supply Chain Risk Management) both address managing cybersecurity risks from the supply chain and third parties.

ISO 27001:2013 A.5.1.1 (policies for information security) and KRITIS §8a(1) BSIG both require documented security policies; KRITIS is sector-specific and focuses on availability and resilience of critical infrastructure.

ISO 27001:2013 A.12.6.1 (technical vulnerability management) and KRITIS §8a(1a) BSIG (attack detection systems) both address detecting and responding to technical threats; KRITIS specifically mandates continuous Angriffserkennung systems.

ISO 27001:2013 A.18.2.1 (independent review of information security) and KRITIS §8a(3) BSIG (proof of compliance every two years) both require independent assessment of security measures, with KRITIS specifically requiring submission of results to BSI.

ISO 27001:2013 A.16.1.2 (reporting information security events) and KRITIS §8b(4a) BSIG (incident reporting to BSI) both address incident reporting obligations; KRITIS additionally mandates external reporting to BSI for significant disruptions.

ISO 27001:2013 A.15.1.3 (information and communication technology supply chain) and KRITIS §8d BSIG (critical components) both address supply chain security; KRITIS specifically requires trustworthiness declarations for critical components from manufacturers.

ISO 27001:2013 A.5.1.1 (policies for information security) and NIS2UmsuCG §30 both require documented risk management measures including security policies; §30 transposes NIS2 Art. 21 into German law for essential entities.

ISO 27001:2013 A.6.1.1 (information security roles and responsibilities) and NIS2UmsuCG §38 (management body responsibility) both require allocation of security responsibilities at senior level; §38 adds personal liability for management failures.

ISO 27001:2013 A.16.1.2 (reporting information security events) and NIS2UmsuCG §35 (incident notification) both require reporting significant incidents; §35 specifies structured timelines (24h early warning, 72h detailed report, 1 month final report) to BSI.

ISO 27001:2013 A.15.1.1 (information security policy for supplier relationships) and NIS2UmsuCG §39 (supply chain security) both require addressing cybersecurity risks from suppliers and service providers.

ISO 27001:2013 A.18.2.1 (independent review of information security) and NIS2UmsuCG §34 (supervisory measures for essential entities) both address independent assessment of security measures, with §34 providing the regulatory enforcement mechanism.

ISO 27001:2013 A.5.1.1 (policies for information security) and BSI IT-Grundschutz ISMS.1 (Security Management) both form the foundational governance requirements of an ISMS, requiring documented security policies approved by management.

ISO 27001:2013 A.6.1.1 (information security roles and responsibilities) and BSI IT-Grundschutz ORP.1 (Organisation) both require clearly defined and allocated security responsibilities within the organisational structure.

ISO 27001:2013 A.7.2.2 (information security awareness, education and training) and BSI IT-Grundschutz ORP.3 (Awareness and Training) both mandate regular and role-appropriate security training and awareness for all personnel.

ISO 27001:2013 A.9.1.1 (access control policy) and BSI IT-Grundschutz ORP.4 (Identity and Access Management) both require a defined access control policy and process governing the granting and revoking of access rights.

ISO 27001:2013 A.10.1.1 (policy on the use of cryptographic controls) and BSI IT-Grundschutz CON.1 (Cryptography Concept) both require an organisation-wide policy on the use of cryptography including algorithm selection and key management.

ISO 27001:2013 A.12.3.1 (information backup) and BSI IT-Grundschutz CON.3 (Data Backup Policy) both require a documented backup policy specifying frequency, retention, storage locations, and regular restoration tests.

ISO 27001:2013 A.18.2.1 (independent review of information security) and BSI IT-Grundschutz DER.3.1 (Audits and Revisions) both require periodic independent audits to verify that security measures are correctly implemented and effective.

ISO 27001:2013 A.13.1.1 (network controls) and BSI IT-Grundschutz NET.1.1 (Network Architecture and Design) both require that network security is designed and implemented with documented controls including network segmentation.

ISO 27001:2022 5.1 (policies for information security) and NIS2UmsuCG §30 both require documented security policies as part of a risk management framework; §30 transposes NIS2 Art. 21(2) into German law for essential entities.

ISO 27001:2022 5.2 (information security roles and responsibilities) and NIS2UmsuCG §38 (management body responsibility) both require that security roles are defined and held accountable; §38 additionally creates personal liability for management.

ISO 27001:2022 5.26 (response to information security incidents) and NIS2UmsuCG §35 both require structured incident response; §35 additionally mandates formal external reporting to BSI within defined timelines.

ISO 27001:2022 5.21 (managing information security in the ICT supply chain) and NIS2UmsuCG §39 (supply chain security) both require that cybersecurity risks from the ICT supply chain are systematically assessed and managed.

ISO 27001:2022 5.35 (independent review of information security) and NIS2UmsuCG §34 (supervisory and enforcement measures) both involve independent assessment of security measures; §34 provides the regulatory enforcement dimension.

ISO 27001:2022 5.31 (legal, statutory, regulatory and contractual requirements) and NIS2UmsuCG §28 (classification of essential and important entities) are related: §28 determines which NIS2UmsuCG obligations apply, which must be identified under the ISO 27001:2022 requirements management control.

GDPR Art. 32 (security of processing) and CIS Control 3 (data protection) both require appropriate technical and organisational measures to protect data, with GDPR specifically focused on personal data and CIS on all organisational data.

GDPR Art. 5(1)(f) (integrity and confidentiality principle) and CIS Control 3 (data protection) both require that data is protected from unauthorised access, disclosure, alteration or destruction through appropriate security controls.

GDPR Art. 33 (notification of personal data breach) and CIS Control 17 (incident response management) both address incident handling; GDPR specifies a 72-hour notification deadline to the supervisory authority for personal data breaches.

GDPR Art. 35 (data protection impact assessment) and CIS Control 18 (penetration testing) are related: security testing performed under CIS 18 can inform the risk assessment required as part of a DPIA under GDPR Art. 35.

GDPR Art. 25 (data protection by design and by default) and CIS Control 16 (application software security) both require that security is embedded in systems and applications at the design stage to minimise data exposure.

GDPR Art. 32 (security of processing) and KRITIS §8a(1) BSIG both require state-of-the-art technical and organisational security measures; GDPR focuses on protection of personal data while KRITIS focuses on availability and resilience of critical infrastructure.

GDPR Art. 33 (notification of personal data breach to supervisory authority) and KRITIS §8b(4a) BSIG (incident reporting to BSI) both impose mandatory breach/incident reporting obligations; they apply to different types of incidents and different reporting authorities.

GDPR Art. 5(1)(f) (integrity and confidentiality principle) and KRITIS §8a(1) BSIG both require that appropriate security measures protect data and systems; KRITIS extends this to the availability and resilience of critical infrastructure services.

GDPR Art. 32 (security of processing) and NIS2UmsuCG §30 (risk management for essential entities) both require appropriate technical and organisational security measures; GDPR focuses on protection of personal data and NIS2UmsuCG on network and information system security.

GDPR Art. 33 (notification of personal data breach) and NIS2UmsuCG §35 (incident notification) both impose reporting obligations with defined timelines; they operate in parallel for incidents involving personal data breaches at NIS2-covered entities.

GDPR Art. 25 (data protection by design and by default) and NIS2UmsuCG §31 (12 minimum security categories) are complementary: the security measures required under §31 support the technical implementation of data protection by design.

GDPR Art. 37 (designation of the data protection officer) and NIS2UmsuCG §38 (management body responsibility) both establish mandatory governance roles; a DPO under GDPR and management accountability under §38 often reside within the same governance structure.

GDPR Art. 5(1)(f) (integrity and confidentiality principle) and BSI IT-Grundschutz ISMS.1 (Security Management) are related: the ISMS established under ISMS.1 provides the overarching security framework within which the GDPR integrity and confidentiality principle is operationalised.

GDPR Art. 32 (security of processing) and BSI IT-Grundschutz ISMS.1 are related: compliance with ISMS.1 and the BSI IT-Grundschutz methodology is widely accepted as evidence of appropriate technical and organisational measures under GDPR Art. 32.
